text:
In particular, the sense of what must be kept “priva te,” of what must be hidden before the

eyes of others, seems to differ strangely from society to society. This is a point that is

frequently made by citing the literature of ethnography, which tells us that there are some

societies in which people cheerfully defecate in full view of others, and at least a few in

which the same is true of having sex. 8 But the same point can be made by citing a large

historical literature, which shows how remarkably ideas of privacy have shifted and

mutated over time. 9 Anyone who wants a vivid example can visit the ruins of Ephesus,

where the modern tourist can set himself down on one of numerous ancient toilet seats, in

a public hall where well- to-do Ephesians gathered to commune with each other, two

thousand years ago, as they collectively emptied their bowels. 10

If “privacy” is a universal human need, which gives rise to a fundamental human

right, why does it take such disconcertingly diverse forms? This is a hard problem for

privacy advocates who want to talk about the values of “personhood,” harder than they

typically acknowledge. It is a hard problem because of the way they usually try to make

8
  Alan Westin, The Origins of Modern Claims to Privacy, in Schoeman ed., Philosophical Dimensions of
Privacy, 62-63; Jean Cohen, Regulating Intimacy: A New Legal Paradigm (Princeton: Princeton U. P.,
2002), 56. For unconvincing doubts about the existence of public defecation, see Barrington Moore,
Privacy: Studies in Social and Cultural History (Armonk, N.Y.: Sharpe, 1984), 65.
9
  For this commonplace, see André Bertrand, Droit à la vie privée et droit à l’image (Paris: Litec, 1999), 2
(citing Jean-Claude Bologne, Histoire de la Pudeur (Paris : Orban, 1986) (public defecation by early-
modern Kings of France) ; Bernard Beignier, La vie privée, in Rémy Cabrillac, Marie -Anne Frison-Roche
and Theirry Revet, Libertés et droits fondamentaux, 5th ed. (Paris : Dalloz, 1999), para. 185 (nude bathing
in Seine and other early modern examples). These historical claims too have stimulated some skepticism,
notably in the attack on Elias of Hans-Peter Duerr, Der Mythos vom Zivilisationsprozess, 5 vols. (Frankfurt
a.M.: Suhrkamp, 1988-2002).
10
   For communal defecation in Greco-Roman antiquity, governed by some complex social and even legal
rules, see Richard Neudecker, Die Pracht der Latrine. Zum Wandel öffentlicher Bedüfnisanstalten in der
kaiserzeitlichen Stadt (München: Pfeil, 1994), 24-39, and for Ephesus in particular, with emphasis on the
posh setting, id. 126-131. For a tourist guide: Peter Scherrer, ed., Ephesos. Der neue Führer (Wien:
Österreiches Archäologisches Institut, 1995), 122.

 their case: Overwhelmingly, privacy advocates rely on what moral philosophers call

“intuitionist” arguments. 11 In their crude form, these sorts of arguments suppose that
